coredata.dc:title: A novel fluorescent biomimetic sensor based on cerium, nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots embedded in cobalt-based metal organic framework@molecularly imprinted polymer for selective and sensitive detection of oxytetracycline
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Xiaohui
Cao, Yichuan
Hu, Xuelian
Cai, Lin
Wang, Haiyang
Fang, Guozhen
Wang, Shuo
coredata.dc:description: Since oxytetracycline (OTC) residues in foodstuff are very harmful to human health, it is urgent to develop an effective assay to monitor OTC. Herein, an innovative fluorescent biomimetic sensor (Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP) based on cerium, nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (Ce, N-CDs) embedded in cobalt-based metal organic framework (ZIF-67) combining with molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) was triumphantly fabricated by a simple one-pot strategy and subsequently applied for the selective and sensitive detection of OTC. In this strategy, the modification of the surface imprint layer enabled the sensor to possess favorable anti-interference ability and specific recognition performance for OTC. The introduction of ZIF-67 with large specific surface area functioning as supporter significantly increased the mass transfer rate and shortened the response time, which improved the adsorption performance and detection sensitivity of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP. Meanwhile, the Ce, N-CDs featured with bright blue fluorescence (FL) and high fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY, 33.69%) were incorporated as a signal label to make the sensor generate measurable FL response signal according to the inner filter effect (IFE), achieving signal amplification. Thanks to the integrated merits of MIP, ZIF-67 and Ce, N-CDs, the constructed Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP sensor not only displayed a favorable linear range (0.05–20 μg mL−1) with a low detection limit (0.015 μg mL−1), but also showed good precision and reproducibility (RSD). More importantly, the designed sensor was successfully used to quantitatively analyze OTC in milk samples, and the corresponding results were in accordance with those obtained by HPLC method. Therefore, the proposed Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP sensor is expected to provide an efficient and sensitive FL detection platform for OTC evaluation in dairy products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cerium, nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots
Cobalt-based metal organic framework
Molecularly imprinted polymer
Oxytetracycline
Fluorescent biomimetic sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of ZIF-67 and Ce, N-CDs
Preparation of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP
Photoluminescence and absorption behavior of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP for OTC
Establishment of OTC FL sensing system
Determination of OTC in practical samples
Precision analysis of proposed FL sensing assay

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety
Tianjin University of Science and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Open Project Program of State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science & Technology (No. SKLFNS-KF-202208).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of (A) ZIF-67, (B) Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and (C) CDs@ZIF-67@NIP. TEM images of (D) ZIF-67, (E) Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and (F) CDs@ZIF-67@NIP. (G) FT-IR spectra and (H) XRD patterns of ZIF-67, Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP. (I) Nitrogen adsorption and desorption curves of ZIF-67, Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67, Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP.

(A) FL emission spectra of Ce, N-CDs, Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67, Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP, and ZIF-67. (B) FL emission spectra of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP, Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP, Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP combined with OTC. (C) FL response of Ce, N-CDs @MIP, Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP to OTC at different incubation times. (D) Effects of pH values (5–9) on the FL intensity of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and the FL response between Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and OTC. The influence of (E) irradiation time at 350 nm and (F) storage period on the FL intensity of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP.

FL spectra of (A) Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and (B) Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP under different concentrations of OTC (excited at 350 nm, OTC concentrations: 0, 0.05, 0.25, 1.25, 2.5, 5, 7.5, 10, 15, 20 μg mL−1). The Stern-Volmer plots of (C) Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and (D) Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP. (E) The UV absorption spectrum of OTC and the FL spectra of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP (Ex, FL excitation spectrum; Em, FL emission spectrum). (F) FL emission decay curves of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP alone and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP with 20 μg mL−1 concentrations of OTC.

(A) The chemical structural formulas of OTC, TET, DOX, CAP and TOB. (B) The FL response (F 0/F) of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP, and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP to 20 μg mL−1 OTC, TET, DOX, CAP and TOB. (C) The influence of TET, DOX, CAP and TOB (40 μg mL−1) on the combination of OTC (20 μg mL−1) and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP. (D) Influence of coexisting substances (100 μg mL−1) on the FL change of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP in the absence or presence of OTC (20 μg mL−1).
The preparation route of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP sensor.
Detection of OTC in milk samples by Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP sensor and HPLC.
Comparison of the constructed FL sensing approach with other reported methods for OTC detection in milk.

head.ce:title: A novel fluorescent biomimetic sensor based on cerium, nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots embedded in cobalt-based metal organic framework@molecularly imprinted polymer for selective and sensitive detection of oxytetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety
Tianjin University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science and Technology, Tianjin 300457, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Hebei Agricultural University, Baoding 071001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Technology, Hebei Agricultural University, Baoding 071001, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The preparation route of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP sensor and the fluorescence sensing process of trace oxytetracycline in milk samples.

Since oxytetracycline (OTC) residues in foodstuff are very harmful to human health, it is urgent to develop an effective assay to monitor OTC. Herein, an innovative fluorescent biomimetic sensor (Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP) based on cerium, nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots (Ce, N-CDs) embedded in cobalt-based metal organic framework (ZIF-67) combining with molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) was triumphantly fabricated by a simple one-pot strategy and subsequently applied for the selective and sensitive detection of OTC. In this strategy, the modification of the surface imprint layer enabled the sensor to possess favorable anti-interference ability and specific recognition performance for OTC. The introduction of ZIF-67 with large specific surface area functioning as supporter significantly increased the mass transfer rate and shortened the response time, which improved the adsorption performance and detection sensitivity of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP. Meanwhile, the Ce, N-CDs featured with bright blue fluorescence (FL) and high fluorescence quantum yield (FLQY, 33.69%) were incorporated as a signal label to make the sensor generate measurable FL response signal according to the inner filter effect (IFE), achieving signal amplification. Thanks to the integrated merits of MIP, ZIF-67 and Ce, N-CDs, the constructed Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP sensor not only displayed a favorable linear range (0.05–20 μg mL−1) with a low detection limit (0.015 μg mL−1), but also showed good precision and reproducibility (RSD). More importantly, the designed sensor was successfully used to quantitatively analyze OTC in milk samples, and the corresponding results were in accordance with those obtained by HPLC method. Therefore, the proposed Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP sensor is expected to provide an efficient and sensitive FL detection platform for OTC evaluation in dairy products.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Cerium, nitrogen co-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent metal organic frameworks (LMOFs), as the late-model organic–inorganic porous materials, are formed via the coordination of organic linkers and atomic clusters or metal ions [1]. In the past decades, LMOFs have shown good application prospects in sensing field because of their remarkable characteristics (e.g. high porosity, large specific surface area and outstanding luminescent properties) [1,2]. Generally speaking, there are two main strategies for the construction of LMOFs. One strategy is utilizing the self-assembly of metal ions and ligands to form the LMOFs with self-luminescence, and this luminescence originates from metal centers, ligands or the charge transfer process between them. The other strategy is to encapsulate guest luminescent species into the host non-luminescent metal organic frameworks (MOFs) based on the inherent porosity of MOFs [3]. For example, Wang et al. reported that the quantum dot (QD)@ MOF core–shell nanocomposites with excellent adsorption ability and outstanding FL sensing performance were acquired by encapsulating CdTe QDs into nanocrystals of the zeolitic imidazolate frameworks (ZIF-8) [4]. Tan et al. synthesized LMOFs by introducing two types of nanoparticles in ZIF-8 for ratiometric FL detection of Cu2+ [5]. Pan et al. developed a new luminescent composite material GQD@ZIF-8 by incorporation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) into ZIF-8 [6]. Although the previously synthesized LMOFs have exhibited their promising future as FL sensors, there are still some limitations, such as the relatively low FL activity in practical applications, which may be attributed to the low FLQY of encapsulated luminescent nanoparticles [7,8]. Consequently, on the premise of basically keeping the original structure and property of MOFs, introducing fluorescent nanoparticles with high FLQY and excellent FL characteristics into MOFs is an important way to build high-performance LMOFs [4,8,9].

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) are a novel type of FL nanoparticles with quasi spherical microstructure, whose size is less than 10 nm [10]. In recent years, due to the excellent properties of CDs, involving stable FL performance, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, uncomplicated preparation and easy modification, they have been widely utilized in temperature sensing [11], biological imaging [12–14], drug delivery [15], photocatalysis [16] and FL sensing [17,18] fields. However, CDs prepared by traditional methods have the shortcoming of relatively low FLQY, making them still challenging in FL sensor field. Fortunately, research has proved that heteroatom doping is a simple and effective way to enhance the FL performance of CDs, thereby obtaining higher FL efficiency [19–21]. After introducing heteroatoms, the intrinsic structure and electron distribution of CDs will be changed, thus affecting their FL properties. Among various non-metallic dopants, nitrogen doping is widely used to acquire CDs with higher FLQY [22,23]. Besides, metal dopants can be utilized to adjust the band structure of CDs and play an important role in enhancing the FL of CDs [19,24]. In our previous work, magnesium, nitrogen double doped CDs with the FLQY of 33.05% were successfully fabricated to detect tetracyclines in food [10,25]. Dewangan et al. reported the cerium and nitrogen double doped CDs (Ce, N-CDs) with exceptional stability for the detection of Hg2+, and the FLQY was as high as 29.4%, which was attributed to high passivity of rare earth element (Ce) combined with nitrogen [26,27]. Zhang et al. prepared the good water-soluble Ce, N-CDs with high FLQY for detection of trace tetracycline and biological imaging [27]. Thus, Ce, N-CDs can be regarded as the promising fluorescent guest materials for constructing LMOFs-based sensing systems in consideration of their high FLQY and good FL stability. Nevertheless, owing to the poor specificity for target molecules, the LMOFs-based sensors are vulnerable to interference from other coexisting substances in complex food detection systems.

Molecular imprinting technology (MIT) has recently attracted immense attention and been employed to artificially synthesize polymers with fixed hole size and specific recognition sites [28]. The generated molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP) not only has high selectivity and recognition ability for the target, but also possesses the merits of strong affinity, favorable absorptivity, superior physical and chemical stability, making it hopeful to become the substitute of natural receptor [29]. Therefore, MIP has a wide-ranging application in various research areas, such as drug delivery [30], chemical and biological sensing [31], separation [28] and catalysis [32]. For now, MIP has been used in LMOFs to construct core–shell molecularly imprinted FL sensors. For example, a novel core–shell FL sensor based on nanostructured MIP-coated UiO-66-NH2 (MIP/Zr-LMOF) was designed to selectively and sensitively detect chloramphenicol [33]. Hu et al. developed a core–shell fluorescent molecularly imprinted nanosensor (Mg,N-CDs@MIL-101@MIP) for specific recognition and ultrasensitive detection of oxytetracycline [7]. In contrast with conventional MIP fabricated by bulk polymerization, LMOFs@MIP with typical core–shell structure displayed higher mass transfer rate and detection sensitivity on account of the existence of LMOFs with large surface area as both supporter and signal transducer [25,34]. Hence, the combination strategy of MIP and LMOFs can offer a sensitive and effective monitoring platform for food security control, such as the detection of trace antibiotic residues.

Oxytetracycline (OTC), a kind of veterinary antibiotics belonging to the tetracycline family, has been increasingly applied in animal husbandry to prevent and treat animal infections caused by pathogenic bacteria in consideration of its low cost and broad-spectrum antibacterial activity [35–37]. However, the excessive use of OTC in animal feed will trigger the antibiotic accumulation in environmental media and animal-derived foods (e.g. milk, meat, egg products). The OTC remaining in the environment will induce the generation of drug-resistant genes and microorganisms [38]. In addition, residual OTC in foodstuffs will eventually enter the human body through consumption and further cause a series of adverse reactions, including allergic syndromes, liver and kidney damage, as well as the decreased human immunity [10,39]. In order to protect public health, both China and the European Union (EU) have set the maximum residue limit (MRL) of OTC in milk (100 μg kg−1 and 225 nM, respectively) [25,40]. To date, numerous analytical techniques, including high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [41], immunoassay [42,43], liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) [44,45], electrochemistry analysis [35,46], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [47] and colorimetry [48], have been performed for the quantitative determination of OTC. Compared with these techniques, FL analysis is taken as a desirable method for OTC detection because of its convenient operation, rapidity, high sensitivity and low-cost [37,49,50]. Therefore, considering the above actuality, it is still imperative and necessary to develop novel, fast, highly sensitive, economical, and effective FL approaches for monitoring trace OTC in food products.

Enlightened by above research and analysis, we constructed a novel FL sensor (Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP) integrating the outstanding luminescence characteristics of CDs, the large specific surface area of MOFs and the high specificity of MIP for the assay of OTC in milk samples. As depicted in Scheme 1 , ZIF-67 with high porosity, brilliant chemical stability and thermostability [51,52] was selected as support (host matrix) and fabricated through self-assembly strategy. The Ce, N-CDs (the guest) with bright blue FL and FLQY up to 33.69% (as fluorophore) were prepared via the hydrothermal method and then modified into ZIF-67 under mild stirring at room temperature to acquire Ce, N-CDs @ZIF-67 compound material. Subsequently, the imprinted layer was synthesized through sol–gel technique by choosing Ce, N-CDs @ZIF-67 as both luminescent nanomaterial and supporter, OTC as template molecule, 3-amino-propyl triethoxysilane (APTES) as functional monomer, and tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) as cross-linker, in which OTC was combined with the functional monomer via hydrogen bond. After removing the template, the 3D cavities matching the shape of target molecular were generated in the imprint layer, meaning the formation of fluorescent imprinted composite (Ce, N-CDs @ZIF-67@ MIP) with high recognition efficiency and selectivity for OTC. During the process of detecting OTC, the blue FL of Ce, N-CDs can be effectively quenched depending on IFE. The existence of ZIF-67 with ideal large superficial area significantly increased the mass transfer rate and shortened the combination balance time, thus improving the adsorption efficiency and detection sensitivity of the sensor. More significantly, the imprinting layer deposited on the surface of ZIF-67 offered specific adsorption and recognition sites for OTC, endowing the sensor with desirable selectivity. In addition, the as-prepared Ce, N-CDs @ZIF-67@ MIP exhibited uniform morphology, excellent photo-stability, superior selectivity and recognition efficiency, as well as ultra-high FL sensitivity, which have been verified by characterizations. Ultimately, the practicability of developed fluorescent sensor has also been confirmed by realizing the selective and sensitive detection of OTC in milk.

Citric acid (CA) and cobalt acetate hexahydrate (Co(NO3)2 6H2O, 99%) were acquired from Aladdin Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Cerium nitrate hexahydrate (Ce(NO3)3 6H2O, 99.5%) was bought from Alfa Aesar Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). 2-methylimidazole (2-MI) was obtained from TCI Chemical Reagent Factory (Shanghai, China). Ethylenediamine (EDA), 3-amino-propyl triethoxysilane (APTES, 98%), tetraethoxysilane (TEOS, 99%), tetracycline (TET), doxycycline (DOX), oxytetracycline (OTC), chloramphenicol (CAP), tobramycin (TOB), cysteine (Cys), tyrosine (Tyr), phenylalanine (Phe), serine (Ser), arginine (Arg), and histidine (His) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Sucrose, glucose, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, MnCl2·4H2O, KCl, CaCl₂, NaCl, ethanol, acetonitrile and methanol (CH3OH, 99.5%) were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Doubly deionized water (DDW,18.2 MΩ cm−1) were obtained by a Milli-Q water purification system. All chemical reagents in the experiment were of analytical grade and used as received.
A detailed description of the instrument was provided in the supplementary information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of ZIF-67 and Ce, N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Ce, N-CDs were prepared on the basis of the hydrothermal method in the previous literature [27]. In brief, 2 g CA and 1 g Ce(NO3)3 6H2O were dissolved in 40 mL ultrapure water, and then 1 mL EDA was added to form a uniform solution under continuous stirring. Subsequently, the mixed solution was reacted in an autoclave at 220℃ for 2 h. After cooling to ambient temperature, the brown-yellow product was further purified by dialysis. Finally, the Ce, N-CDs powder obtained by lyophilization was sealed and stored at 4℃ for following experiments.

ZIF-67 was synthetized according to the reported literature with some modifications [51]. Firstly, 1.16 g (4.0 mmol) of Co(NO3)2 6H2O and 1.31 g (16.0 mmol) 2-MI were dispersed in 30 mL methanol, respectively. Whereafter, the above two solutions were mixed evenly, magnetically stirred for 1 h, and quietly maintained for 24 h. Finally, the resulting purple precipitate was collected through centrifugation (8500 rpm, 10 min), washed 3 times with methanol solution and dried in a vacuum oven (50℃) for 8 h.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP
ce:section.ce:para: Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP was fabricated in accordance with sol–gel process in our previous work [25,53]. First, 30 mg of the as-prepared ZIF-67 particles were uniformly dispersed in 28 mL of ethanol under sonication. Then 50 mg of Ce, N-CDs were added into above solution and kept constantly magnetic stirring for 1 h. Next, OTC (0.1 mmol), APTES (0.8 mmol), TEOS (2 mmol) and HOAc (2 mL, 0.1 mmol L-1) were successively added to the reactive system and stirred for 10 h. The obtained polymeric substance was collected by centrifuging. After several washings with ethanol for removal of unreacted reagents, OTC imprinted polymer was carefully eluted using a mixture of water and methanol (v/v, 1:1) until the target molecule was not detected by the UV–vis spectrophotometer, followed by vacuum-drying at 50℃ for 6 h. Simultaneously, as comparison materials, the non-imprinted polymer (Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP) and cerium-nitrogen co-doped CDs-embedded molecularly imprinted polymer (Ce, N-CDs @MIP) were synthesized following the same steps without adding the template molecule OTC and ZIF-67, respectively.

The typical FL determination procedure for OTC was implemented as follows. First, 1 mg of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP was dispersed in 2 mL of PBS buffer solution (pH = 7) to acquire Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP suspension (0.5 mg mL−1). Then different concentrations of OTC standards were mixed with Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP suspension (v/v, 1:1). After incubating for 18 min, the emission spectra of the mixture from 380 nm to 580 nm at the excitation wavelength of 350 nm were obtained and the FL emission intensity at 445 nm was recorded. The selective identification performance of constructed FL sensor was assessed by introducing other structural analogues or potential interfering substances in the presence or absence of OTC under the given experimental conditions as above. Three parallel measurements were conducted to ensure the accuracy of the test results.

Fresh milk samples purchased from local supermarket were chosen for practical sample analysis to evaluate the feasibility of the developed FL sensor in this work and the spiked recovery experiments were accomplished. The spiked samples containing OTC standards of 0, 1, 5, and 15 μg mL−1 were prepared and placed overnight. Then the pretreatment of milk samples was carried out in accordance with the previously reported literature [10]. Briefly speaking, 2 mL of Na2EDTA-Mcllvaine buffer solution was first added into a 15 mL centrifuge tube containing 1 mL of milk sample. After vortically mixed for 3 min, the mixture was centrifuged (10℃, 8000 rpm, 15 min) to separate the deposition and retain the supernatant. Whereafter, the above extraction steps were repeated. The twice supernatants of samples were merged and filtrated with a 0.22-μM microporous membrane. At last, the collected extract solution was blown dry with N2, and then redispersed in 1 mL PBS buffer solution (pH = 7) for subsequent FL detection.

In addition, for the purpose of verifying the reliability and accuracy of FL analysis method, the conventional HPLC assay was also utilized to monitor OTC in milk samples. The milk pretreatment and sample analytical process were designed and executed according to the Chinese National Standard (GB/T 22990–2008). The experimental operating parameters of HPLC detection system were presented in the supplementary information.

In order to acquire polymers with desirable binding affinity and splendid FL response to OTC, the influence of synthetic conditions (including the molar ratio of OTC, APTES and TEOS, the addition amount of Ce, N-CDs and polymerization time) on the comprehensive performance of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP was studied, and the optimization data were shown in Tables S1–S3.

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) and transmission electron microscope (TEM) were used to investigate the morphologies of Ce, N-CDs, ZIF-67, Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67, Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP. As previously reported [26,27], the synthesized Ce, N-CDs presented a uniform spherical morphology with an average diameter of 3.35 nm, which was shown in Fig. S1A and B. Meantime, the element mapping image in Fig. S1C plainly showed that C, N, O, and Ce elements were evenly distributed in Ce, N-CDs, further illustrating the triumphant formation of Ce, N-CDs. Fig. 1 A and D displayed the SEM and TEM images of ZIF-67 nanomaterials, exhibiting a typical, regular and well dispersed dodecahedron structure with a size of about 500 nm (Fig. S2A), and a smooth surface, which were compatible with previous researches [51,52]. The TEM and the corresponding element mapping images of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67 (Fig.S3) clearly identified the even distribution of the main elements (C, N, O, Co and Ce) in the composite, indicating the successful synthesis of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67. Both Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP (Fig. 1B and E) and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP (Fig. 1C and F) had a classic core–shell structure and still retained the skeleton frame of ZIF-67. Moreover, compared with ZIF-67, they possessed rough porous surface structure formed during polymerization, and their particle sizes were increased to nearly 550 nm (Fig. S2B and C), preliminarily proving the synthesis of polymers. Besides, the element mapping image of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP (Fig.S4) further verified that the molecular imprinted layer was triumphantly deposited on the surface of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were applied to study the surface chemistry of ZIF-67, Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP. As displayed in Fig. 1G, the characteristic peak at 426 cm−1 represented the Co-N bonding in ZIF-67 [54,55], and the absorption bands appeared at 650–1450 cm−1 were ascribed to the stretching and plane bending of imidazole ring in ligand 2-MI [52]. Moreover, the peak at 1579 cm−1 was formed by the stretching vibration mode of C = N bond in 2-MI [52,54]. And the two absorption peaks of 2929 and 3134 cm−1 were derived from the C-H stretching patterns of aromatic ring and the aliphatic chain in ligand, respectively [52]. These characteristic peaks were observed in the spectra of ZIF-67, Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP, indicating that ZIF-67 was triumphantly prepared and introduced into the composite materials. At the same time, the N-H (3415 and 1656 cm−1) and O-H (3320 cm−1) characteristic peaks related to Ce, N-CDs all appeared in the Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP curves, signifying the successful embedding of Ce, N-CDs in the composite [25,27,56]. Additionally, the peak values of 1046, 797 and 465 cm−1 were corresponding to the Si-O-Si and Si-O stretching vibration, validating the existence of APTES and TEOS [51]. Apparently, the characteristic peaks of ZIF-67 were weakened in Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP spectra, which implied that the imprinted layer was successfully coated on the appearance of ZIF-67 [25,34]. The above data illustrated the successful synthesis of fluorescent sensor (Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of ZIF-67, Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP were measured and revealed in Fig. 1H. The XRD pattern of bare ZIF-67 was matched well with the previously reported diffraction data, and the diffraction peaks mainly appeared in the 2θ range of 5-50°, demonstrating that ZIF-67 nanocrystals were synthesized with success [57,58]. After the decoration of imprinted coating, the main characteristic peak signals of ZIF-67 in Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP were weakened, which manifested that MIP was triumphantly modified on ZIF-67 by MIT while maintaining the original crystallographic structure of ZIF-67 [51,59].

The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface areas of resulting materials were determined via N2 adsorption–desorption survey. As depicted in Fig. 1I, the specific surface areas of ZIF-67, Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67, Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP were 1658.69 m2 g−1, 568.10 m2 g−1, 12.03 m2 g−1 and 7.05 m2 g−1, respectively. Clearly, compared with ZIF-67, the surface area of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67 was significantly reduced, certificating that Ce, N-CDs were successfully embedded in ZIF-67 [8,25]. The specific surface areas of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP were both smaller than that of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67 owing to the existence of the surface imprint layer [25,60]. Concurrently, Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP possessed a slightly larger specific surface area than Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP, which was attributed to the remaining imprinted cavities after removing the template OTC [8].
In conclusion, these results suggested that the fluorescent biomimetic sensor based on Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP was successfully established.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photoluminescence and absorption behavior of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP for OTC
ce:section.ce:para: First of all, the FL characteristics of obtained fluorescent biomimetic sensor were explored. As clarified in Fig. 2 A, the FL spectrum of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67 showed no apparent difference with that of Ce, N-CDs, and ZIF-67 had no obvious FL emission signal at 445 nm, which manifested that the embedding of Ce, N-CDs into ZIF-67 was unable to affect the luminescence performance of Ce, N-CDs, and the FL properties of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67 and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP were both derived from Ce, N-CDs. Interestingly, thanks to the wrapping of surface imprinted coating, Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP exhibited a vaguely lower FL emission intensity at 445 nm than Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67, but it still featured a strong FL intensity (445 nm) consistent with Ce, N-CDs, suggesting that the introduction of MIP and ZIF-67 had no significant impact on the FL performance of Ce, N-CDs. All data mentioned above attested that the developed Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP sensor retained the distinctive luminous performance of Ce, N-CDs.

Secondly, the viability of fluorescent biomimetic sensor was also tested by comparing the FL response of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP to the target molecule. As unveiled in Fig. 2B, visibly, when OTC was present in the sensing system, the FL intensities of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP at 445 nm were both validly decreased. However, Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP showed more obvious FL change than Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP because of the introduction of specific binding sites for OTC in Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP. After the elimination of OTC, the FL intensities of the polymeric substances at 445 nm were recovered. More importantly, the FL spectrum of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP was basically similar to that of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP, implying that the established Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP FL sensing assay was viable to detect OTC.

Subsequently, adsorption kinetics tests of Ce, N-CDs @MIP, Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP were accomplished to discuss the adsorbing performance of synthesized polymers. As presented in Fig. 2C, the changes of FL quenching signals at 445 nm (F 0/F) during the reaction time from 0 to 30 min were recorded. With the prolongation of incubation time, the F 0/F index of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP increased sharply at first, and subsequently tended to be invariable after 18 min, meaning that the combination between OTC and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP reached a balance in 18 min. Therefore, 18 min was chosen for the best reaction time in follow-up experiments. At the same time, the change tendency of FL signal of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP was extremely consistent with that of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP, but Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP exhibited faster adsorption rate and better adsorptive capacity for OTC than Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP under the same response time because the presence of imprinting sites enhanced the binding ability of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP to OTC. Notably, compared with Ce, N-CDs @MIP, Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP had more splendid adsorption features meanwhile the adsorption equilibrium time was shortened from 27 min to 18 min, which indicated that the incorporation of ZIF-67 with large surface area provided a platform for the formation of molecular imprinted layer to allow more imprinted sites on the surface to specifically bind with OTC, thus improving the mass transfer rate and combination affinity [8,34].

Moreover, the effects of pH (5–9) in reactive system on the FL intensity of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP as well as the FL quenching response (F 0/F) of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP to OTC were explored in the absence and presence of OTC, respectively. As enunciated in Fig. 2D, the FL of the proposed sensor appeared an acceptable slight change with the pH value increasing from 5 to 9, showing that the constructed FL sensor had decent stability within a wide pH range. Noticeably, when OTC existed in the sensing system, the FL response variable reached the maximum at pH 7, emanating that Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP had the highest adsorption combination efficiency to the target molecule. Consequently, pH 7 was regarded as the optimal pH in reaction process.

Eventually, the optical stability of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP sensor was explored by recording the FL intensity at 445 nm under different ultraviolet irradiation time and storage time. As manifested in Fig. 2E, as the UV irradiation (350 nm) time increased, the FL intensity of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP was basically unchanged, indicating that the proposed FL sensor possessed gratifying photo-bleaching resistance. Meanwhile, within 60 days (Fig. 2F), the FL of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP did not decrease markedly, which denoted that the synthesized sensor had a good FL stability.
The above results testified that Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP sensor featured excellent photoluminescence and adsorption properties, and was expected to be a prospective material for FL detection of OTC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Establishment of OTC FL sensing system
ce:section.ce:para:
Under the optimal experimental conditions, for the sake of quantitative analysis of OTC, the dosage-dependent FL characteristics of the developed FL sensor were investigated via capturing and analyzing FL spectra of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP after adding various concentrations of OTC. Obviously, with the gradual increase of OTC concentration, the FL intensity of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP at 445 nm under 350 nm excitation decreased progressively (Fig. 3 A), and the FL quenching signal (F 0/F) of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP showed an outstanding linear relationship with the OTC concentration in the range of 0.05–20 μg mL−1 (Fig. 3C). And the LOD was estimated to be 0.015 μg mL−1 in the light of the standard 3S b /slope (S b was the standard deviation of nine blank measurements) [10,61]. The FL quenching response can be depicted by Stern-Volmer equation [49,53]:
F o
/ F = 1 +
K
SV
C
orc
where F 0 and F represent the FL intensities before and after the addition of OTC, respectively, K SV is the quenching constant (M−1), and C OTC is the concentration of OTC. Here, the imprinting factor (IF, defined as the specific value of K SV, Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP to K SV, Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP) can be employed as a crucial indicator to appraise the adsorption binding properties of the sensor. Compared with Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP (Fig. 3B), Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP exhibited more prominent FL quenching change at the same addition amount of OTC, illustrating that the existence of imprinted recognition sites improved the adsorption and combination efficiency of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP towards OTC. Simultaneously, the slopes of corresponding standard linear equations of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP (F 0 /F = 0.0777C OTC + 1.0680, R 2 = 0.998) and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP (F0/F = 0.0301COTC + 1.0197, R2 = 0.989) were compared (Fig. 3C and D), explicating that Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP sensor held higher selectivity and identification ability for OTC. The highest IF was calculated as 2.62, which further proved that Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP had exceptional affinity for OTC. Accordingly, the Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP sensing system created in this paper was suitable for quantitative detection of OTC.

Besides, in order to better explain the FL quenching phenomenon, a profound study was made to investigate and infer the potential FL sensing mechanism between Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and OTC. The ultraviolet absorption spectrum of OTC and the FL spectra of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP were first provided in Fig. 3E. Clearly, there was a partial overlap between the UV absorption band of OTC and the excitation spectrum of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP, preliminarily determining the possibility of IFE [40,49]. Substantially, the FL lifetime of the fluorophore was immutable if IFE occurred, but it reduced in the FL resonance energy transfer (FRET) process [25]. As a result, the FL lifetime was taken as an important parameter to further explore the possible FL quenching mechanism and the FL emission decay curves of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP before and after the introduction of OTC were plotted in Fig. 3F. It was worthwhile to note that the FL lifetime of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP presented inconspicuous change after combining with OTC, which thereby substantiated that the FL quenching mechanism caused by OTC was IFE instead of FRET.

The selectivity of developed FL sensor was assessed by selecting compounds (TET, DOX, CAP and TOB) with similar structures to the target OTC as interfering substances. The chemical structural formulas of OTC and its analogues were displayed in Fig. 4 A. As revealed in Fig. 4B, the FL response of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP to OTC was remarkably higher than that to other analytes, which was largely due to the existence of recognition sites that fully matched the size, shape and functional groups of OTC in Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP. Therefore, the prepared probe showed excellent recognition ability to OTC. Nevertheless, the FL response of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP towards OTC was as low as that towards other analogues, which was attributed to the nonspecific physical adsorption between Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@NIP and these substances. The above results declared that the constructed Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP probe had excellent selectivity and specificity for OTC.

The competitive adsorption tests were also performed by adding different analogues (TET, DOX, CAP and TOB) to solutions containing OTC and Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP to evaluate the anti-interference ability of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP. Fig. 4C manifested that even if the concentration of the analogues increased to 40 μg mL−1 (twice the concentration of OTC), the FL change of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP in the mixed solution was inconspicuous, implying that the structural analogues had little impact on the binding between Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and OTC owing to the introduction of specific imprinting sites. Accordingly, the Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP sensor appeared higher affinity and better selectivity towards OTC during adsorption process.

Moreover, in the practical detection process, the possible coexisting interfering substances originated from sophisticated food matrices, including common amino acids (Cys, Tyr, Phe, Ser, Arg and His), ions (K+, Na+, Cl-, NO3 –, Ca2+, Mn2+, Mg2+ and Zn2+), and sugars (glucose and sucrose), might affect the exactitude of the experimental results. Thus, to further validate the recognition specificity and feasibility of the prepared Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP sensor in the complex environment, the effects of aforementioned interfering substances on the FL sensing system were also investigated. As depicted in Fig. 4D, even when the concentration of interfering species was as high as 100 μg mL−1 (5 times the concentration of OTC) in the system, their influence on the FL variation of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP and FL monitoring of OTC was negligible. These gratifying results proved that Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP maintained favorable anti-interference performance under complicated conditions, which provided a promising means for quantitative analysis of OTC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of OTC in practical samples
ce:section.ce:para: For the sake of further authenticating the applicability of the established Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP FL sensing assay, milk purchased from the local supermarket was selected for real sample analysis. And no OTC was measured in blank samples by proposed FL method and conventional high performance liquid chromatography. As a consequence, the spiking and recovery trials were implemented by analyzing spiked milk samples containing final OTC concentrations of 0, 1, 5 and 15 μg mL−1. As clearly listed in Table 1 , the FL sensing method based on Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP had a satisfactory recovery (96.05%-99.64%) with a relative standard deviation of less than 3.19%. Plainly, these results were highly consistent with that determined by traditional HPLC, confirming that the constructed FL approach had satisfying accuracy and reliability for the quantitative determination of OTC in dairy products.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Precision analysis of proposed FL sensing assay
ce:section.ce:para: The intra- and inter-day precisions of the designed FL assay in this work were assessed with spiked samples of OTC standards at three different levels (1, 5 and 15 μg mL−1), and the relevant findings were summarized in Table S4. Apparently, the resulting relative standard deviations (RSDs) were 0.64%-3.23% and 0.86%-3.56% (n = 6), respectively, demonstrating that the Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP sensor had good stability, ideal accuracy and reproducibility, making it feasible to detect OTC in actual samples.

The performance comparison between Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP sensing assay and other existing approaches for OTC detection was presented in Table 2 . It was found that the method developed in this paper exhibited outstanding performance, including a lower LOD, good precision (RSD), comparable linear range, and satisfying recoveries, which benefited from the following advantages. Firstly, due to the unique optical properties of Ce, N-CDs (such as high FLQY and excellent photo-stability), the prepared Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP showed a sensitive FL response to OTC, which was the prerequisite for detecting OTC. Secondly, the introduction of ZIF-67 with high porosity and surface area as a carrier not only reduced the aggregation quenching of Ce, N-CDs and improved its dispersion, thus endowing the FL sensor with good stability, but also evidently increased the mass transfer rate and adsorption efficiency of the constructed sensor, thereby enhancing the sensing sensitivity. Thirdly, the decoration of the surface imprint layer enabled the sensor to accurately and quickly recognize the target molecules in the complicated sample matrix, which was conducive to shortening the detection and analysis time. Thus, the Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP sensor displayed great application potential in the OTC detection field.

In summary, a novel fluorescent sensor based on Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP was successfully synthesized for the first time via a simple one-pot strategy. The incorporation of Ce, N-CDs with exceptional FL characteristics as fluorophores endowed the prepared Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP a sensitive FL response to OTC through the efficient IFE sensing mechanism. Because of the modification of the surface imprint layer, the sensor possessed favorable anti-interference capability and specific recognition performance to target molecules in complicated detection environment. Furthermore, the introduction of ZIF-67 with large specific surface area as carrier not only further improved the FL stability and adsorption capacity of Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP sensor, but also shortened the sample analysis time. Owing to the integrated advantages of CDs, MIP and MOFs, the obtained Ce, N-CDs@ZIF-67@MIP sensor had the merits of low detection limit, wide linear range, as well as desirable selectivity and sensitivity. Finally, the constructed FL sensor has been triumphantly employed for the quantitative determination of OTC in milk along with satisfying recoveries. Hence, our work enriches the construction strategy of fluorescent sensors for OTC monitoring, and provides a sensitive detection platform for OTC evaluation in practical samples.

Xiaohui Wang: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yichuan Cao: Formal analysis. Xuelian Hu: Methodology, Validation. Lin Cai: Investigation. Haiyang Wang: Validation. Guozhen Fang: Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Shuo Wang: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Open Project Program of State Key Laboratory of Food Nutrition and Safety, Tianjin University of Science & Technology (No. SKLFNS-KF-202208).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2023.108606.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
